skill.name: hig-foundations
skill.description: Guides Apple platform design work using Human Interface Guidelines foundations for color, typography, layout, accessibility, privacy, motion, icons, and related UI decisions.
skill.summary: Apply Apple HIG foundations to product, interface, and implementation decisions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical combination because the Markdown references contain many URLs, inline backticks, color values, and privacy terms. Manual review found documentation content rather than executable code, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection. The only retained concern is a low-risk instruction to check a specific local design context file before asking questions.

low_findings.title: Local Context File Read Instruction
low_findings.description: The skill asks the model to check `.claude/apple-design-context.md` before asking questions. This is a bounded local project-context read, not exfiltration, but it is a filesystem access instruction that users should understand.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and limited to one local context file. I found no network transmission or instruction to read broad sensitive paths.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution occurs in Markdown inline-code formatting and documentation links, such as API names and color symbols. No executable script, shell invocation, or user-controlled command path was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are Markdown backticks around documentation terms, not code execution syntax in a runnable file. The skill package contains Markdown references only.

low_findings.title: Static Network Findings Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Apple Developer documentation, Apple-hosted images, and the author attribution link. They are reference links in Markdown rather than programmatic outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear as Markdown link targets and image sources. I found no fetch, curl, webhook, or other outbound network execution path.

low_findings.title: Credential and Keychain Findings Are Privacy Guidance
low_findings.description: The privacy reference discusses passwords, passkeys, Keychain services, and authentication as HIG guidance. It does not access secrets, environment variables, macOS Keychain APIs, or credential stores.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are prose recommending secure storage and system authentication. There is no executable code or API call that reads credentials.

content.value_statement: Apple platform design decisions require detailed guidance across accessibility, layout, color, motion, writing, and privacy. This skill organizes HIG foundation references into practical guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Apple HIG foundation guidance for color, typography, layout, motion, materials, and dark mode.
Connects accessibility topics such as VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, contrast, Reduce Motion, and Switch Control to design decisions.
Provides platform-aware guidance for app icons, SF Symbols, images, RTL layouts, privacy, and inclusive design.
Cites the relevant local reference file and section when giving HIG recommendations.
Suggests concrete SwiftUI, UIKit, or AppKit implementation patterns when requested.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not include executable validation tools.
It summarizes bundled reference files and may not include newer Apple guidance after the source snapshot.
It cannot verify App Store review outcomes or legal privacy compliance.
It needs target platform details for precise recommendations.

use_cases.title: Review an iOS interface concept
use_cases.description: Check a mobile screen against HIG foundations for layout, color, accessibility, iconography, and copy before implementation.

use_cases.title: Translate design guidance into code
use_cases.description: Map HIG recommendations to SwiftUI, UIKit, or AppKit patterns for colors, type styles, materials, and adaptive layouts.

use_cases.title: Prepare an accessibility pass
use_cases.description: Identify improvements for VoiceOver labels, Dynamic Type behavior, contrast, Reduce Motion alternatives, and inclusive language.

prompt_templates.title: Check a screen concept
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Apple platform screen using HIG foundations. Focus on color, typography, layout, accessibility, icons, and privacy. Cite the relevant reference sections.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a SwiftUI implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SwiftUI view for HIG alignment. Suggest changes for semantic colors, Dynamic Type, layout spacing, SF Symbols, materials, and Reduce Motion support.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a design system component
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this reusable Apple platform component against HIG foundations. Cover accessibility states, dark mode, localization, RTL support, icon weight, motion, and privacy impact.

prompt_templates.title: Create platform-specific guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Create HIG-based recommendations for this feature across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, and visionOS. Explain platform differences and implementation patterns.

output_examples.input: Review a custom settings screen for an iOS app.
output: Use semantic background and label colors instead of fixed color values.
Keep row spacing consistent with platform settings patterns.
Add VoiceOver labels for icon-only controls and test large Dynamic Type sizes.
Cite color, layout, accessibility, and writing references for each recommendation.

output_examples.input: Suggest a privacy permission flow for photo upload.
output: Ask for photo access only when the user starts the upload task.
Use a concise purpose string that explains the feature benefit.
Avoid custom screens that imitate the system permission alert.
Prefer limited photo access when the feature does not need the full library.

output_examples.input: Make a dashboard work better in dark mode and high contrast.
output: Replace decorative fixed colors with semantic system colors where possible.
Provide dark and increased-contrast variants for custom brand colors.
Avoid using color alone for status. Add text labels or symbols.
Check contrast for text, icons, separators, and chart marks.

best_practices: Start each request with the target platforms, user task, and current design constraints.
Ask for citations to specific reference files and sections when making design decisions.
Validate recommendations with accessibility, localization, dark mode, and privacy requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for generic Apple-like visuals without naming the target platform and feature context.
Do not use fixed colors, fixed text sizes, or icon-only controls without accessibility checks.
Do not treat the skill as a substitute for App Store policy, legal privacy review, or current Apple documentation.

faq.question: What does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Apple HIG foundations including color, typography, layout, accessibility, icons, motion, materials, privacy, inclusion, and RTL support.

faq.question: Can it write SwiftUI code?
faq.answer: It can suggest SwiftUI, UIKit, and AppKit patterns, but it is mainly a design guidance skill.

faq.question: Does it replace Apple documentation?
faq.answer: No. It uses bundled reference snapshots and should be checked against current Apple documentation for final decisions.

faq.question: Can it audit accessibility?
faq.answer: It can guide accessibility review for VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, contrast, motion, and control labeling. It does not run automated tests.

faq.question: Is it useful for Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. Claude Code and Codex can use it to connect Apple design guidance to implementation changes.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide target platforms, screen or component context, brand constraints, accessibility goals, and any existing implementation.
